meta.title: Elena Voss — The Cartographer's Daughter | Literary Fiction
meta.description: Elena Voss's debut novel maps grief, inheritance, and the lines we draw between belonging and escape. Pre-order The Cartographer's Daughter now.

hero.headline: Elena Voss
hero.body: A daughter inherits her father's maps and discovers the one place he refused to chart. A novel about borders, memory, and the geography of grief.

about.headline: Before words, there were maps.
about.body: Elena Voss studied cartography at the University of Edinburgh before she understood that she was really studying how people decide what to leave out. She spent a decade making maps of places that no longer exist. The Cartographer's Daughter is her first novel.

items.title: The Cartographer's Daughter
items.tagline: A daughter inherits her father's maps and discovers the one place he refused to chart.

items.title: Meridian
items.tagline: Two surveyors. One disputed border. The summer that divided them both.

items.title: The Last Sounding
items.tagline: She mapped the seafloor for thirty years. Her daughter found the charts.

items.title: Minor Territories
items.tagline: A novel about the places that appear on no map.

items.title: A house full of secrets
items.body: When Mara inherits her estranged father's Edinburgh flat, she finds 300 hand-drawn maps covering every wall except one.

items.title: Two timelines, one truth
items.body: The novel moves between 1970s Leningrad and present-day Scotland, tracing what a single act of erasure costs across generations.

items.title: Language as landscape
items.body: Voss writes sentences the way a surveyor lays out ground: precise, unhurried, and always in service of the full picture.

items.title: No easy resolutions
items.body: This is a novel that trusts its readers enough to leave certain borders undrawn.

pullquote.quote: Grief, geography, and the terrible weight of things inherited. Quiet, devastating, essential.

blurbs.quote: Voss writes with the patience and precision of a cartographer. Every sentence earns its place on the page.
One of the most assured debut voices I've read this year. The Cartographer's Daughter asks hard questions and lets them breathe.
Grief, geography, and the terrible weight of things inherited. Quiet, devastating, essential.
A debut that reads like a fifth novel. Voss knows exactly where she is going, and she takes you with her.
The kind of book that makes you want to call your father. Or forgive him. Possibly both.
Rich, intelligent, and impossible to put down once you cross the threshold of the first chapter.

author_strip.headline: The story behind the story.
author_strip.subheadline: Elena spent a decade making maps of places that no longer exist.
author_strip.paragraph_1: The Cartographer's Daughter grew from a single image: a woman standing in front of a building, in a city whose name has been erased from official records. That image became Mara's inheritance, and Mara's search became the novel. Elena draws on her background in cartography—the study of how societies decide what to include and, more importantly, what to leave out.
author_strip.paragraph_2: This is a novel about borders, both literal and inherited. About the maps we inherit from our parents, and the blank spaces we must learn to read. About what happens when geography becomes autobiography, and when the act of mapping becomes an act of resistance.

newsletter.headline: First to know.
newsletter.body: Join the list for ARC access, early chapter previews, and updates on Elena's next book before they go anywhere else.

book_pages.title: The Map Room
lines_left: he maps covered every wall of the flat.
They had been there, Mara assumed, for
decades — some of them curling at the
corners, their colours faded to the amber
tones of old photographs. She stood in the
doorway with her coat still on and her keys
in her hand, unable to step inside, as though
the flat knew she did not belong there.
The smell of the place — cedar and cold ash
and something metallic she could not name —
pressed itself against her face like a held-back
apology. Her father had left her the flat in his
will, along with the maps and a key to a safety-
deposit box she had not yet opened.
lines_right: Her father had not said, in any of his letters,
that he kept maps. He had written about the
weather in Edinburgh. About the quality of
the light in November. About a particular stone
bridge he could see from the window. He had
never written about the maps.
He had never written about her mother.
He had never written about the eight hundred
miles of sea and time that had grown between
them like a geography no cartographer had yet
agreed to chart.
Mara set her keys on the windowsill, because
that seemed like the thing to do, and began
to look more carefully at the walls.

book_pages.title: The Box
lines_left: he bank was on George Street, which was
the kind of address her father would have
chosen. He had a fondness for formality.
She remembered this about him from the
letters: the way he always wrote the date
in full — the twelfth of October, not 12/10.
The way he signed off not with love, but
with your affectionate father, as though
affection were a thing that required a title.
The clerk brought out the box on a tray,
as if it were something fragile or valuable.
It was the size of a shoebox. It was heavier
than Mara expected. She carried it to a
private room and sat with it for a long
time before she lifted the lid.
lines_right: Inside: a map she did not recognise.
Not one of the published kind — not a
survey sheet or an atlas page, but
something hand-drawn, on paper that had
yellowed at the edges. A coastline she
could not name. Interior features marked
in her father's hand. A river. A village.
A name, written and then crossed out.
Below the map: a photograph.
A woman she did not recognise, standing
in front of a building she did not know,
in a city she could not identify.
On the back, in her father's hand:
Before the erasure. 1971.

book_pages.title: The Line
lines_left: he archive was in a building that looked
like a library from the outside and like
a bunker from the inside. Mara waited
forty minutes at the desk while the
archivist located the relevant folder.
When it arrived, the folder was thin.
Five pages. Official language. Official
silence in every line.
She had come expecting documents.
What she found instead was an absence:
three years in the early 1970s where
her father's name simply ceased to appear
in any record. No employment. No address.
No correspondence. A gap in the official
geography of his life.
lines_right: The archivist, when Mara asked, said that
such gaps were not uncommon. Sometimes
people moved. Sometimes records were
lost. Sometimes, he said carefully,
looking at his desk, people chose to
leave certain parts of themselves
off the map.
Mara thought about the name on the
back of the photograph. She thought
about the crossed-out words on the
hand-drawn coastline. She thought about
her father writing about the weather,
year after year, while somewhere in his
past a whole country was going dark.

book_pages.title: The Sea
lines_left: he crossing took eleven hours. Mara sat
on the upper deck with her coat pulled
close and watched the Scottish coast
disappear, and then, much later, watched
another coast appear. The sea between
them was grey and indifferent and
completely without memory, which she
found, unexpectedly, a relief.
She had her father's map in her bag.
She had the photograph. She had an
address, written in her own hand from
her own research, that might or might
not still exist. She had no plan beyond
arriving and seeing what was there.
lines_right: Her father had mapped this crossing once.
She found the page among the others in
the flat: a simple sheet, the outlines
of two coastlines, and between them
only water — no shipping lanes, no
soundings, no notation at all.
Just the distance.
She understood now why he had left
the interior blank. Some geographies
resist annotation. Some distances
can only be measured by crossing them.
And some places on the map exist only
in the act of going, not in the
certainty of arrival.

items.a: The Cartographer's Daughter publishes in autumn 2026. Join the list to get the exact date and access to early chapters before the general announcement.
Yes. The audiobook will be released simultaneously with the print edition and is available for pre-order through all major platforms.

items.q: Can I request an advance reading copy?
items.a: ARC requests are open to booksellers, librarians, and journalists. Send a short note via the contact form and Elena's team will respond within five working days.

items.q: Will there be a signed edition?
items.a: A limited signed and numbered first edition will be available exclusively through independent bookshops. Details go to the list first.

items.q: Is this part of a series?
items.a: The Cartographer's Daughter is a standalone novel. Elena is currently working on a second book, though the two are not sequels.

footer.tagline: Some stories need a map. Some maps are the story.
